front: Ruler: founder of a namesake variant of Marxism-Leninism whose quotations were collected in the Little Red Book
Ruler: first Chairman of the People's Republic of China, whose founding he declared on October 1, 1949
Ruler: this leader's portrait hangs over Tiananmen Square
Ruler: took power from Bo Gu and Otto Braun, the "returned student faction," at the Zunyi Conference during the Long March
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Ruler: narrated his autobiography to journalist Edgar Snow in Yan'an, published as Red Star Over China
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Ruler: allied with his rival Chiang Kai-shek against Japan following the Xi'an Incident, forming the Second United Front
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Ruler: developed theories of protracted guerrilla warfare while living in a cave
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Work: this 1937 book compares the guerrilla to "a fish who swims within the sea of the people"
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Ruler: declared China would "lean to one side" and wrote On New Democracy
work: Rise to Power: The Long March & the Second United Front

front: Philosopher: in the essay "On Contradiction," compared opposing classes to a bomb that is "a single entity in which opposites coexist"
Philosopher: originated the phrase "political power grows out of the barrel of a gun"
Philosopher: also coined the phrase "women hold up half the sky"
Philosopher: divided the world into superpowers, developing nations, and exploited nations in his "Three Worlds Theory"
Philosopher: delivered the Talks at the Yan'an Forum on Literature and Art
Philosopher: wrote an essay praising Norman Bethune, a man he had met only once, urging readers to learn his "spirit of absolute selflessness"
Philosopher: retold the folk tale of a Foolish Old Man who moved mountains, substituting "the masses of the people" for God
Philosopher: used the metaphor of a bucket of water that must be raised from the ground to argue for raising literary standards
Work: this collection of Mao's quotations was compiled and published by Lin Biao, who praised Mao as a genius
Work: includes sections on "Democracy in the Three Main Fields" and "Criticism and Self-Criticism"
Work: its "Mass Line" section states that "the people alone are the motive force in world history"
Work: its portrait of Mao became the basis for Andy Warhol's series of silkscreen paintings
Event: in 1956, this leader encouraged citizens to voice their true opinions of the government, comparing artistic and scientific flourishing to "a hundred flowers blooming"
Event: campaign against the "Four Pests" (including sparrows) contributed to a famine remembered as the "three years of natural disasters"
Event: overreporting of crops planted via "deep plowing" was tied to his "General Line" concept, one of the Three Red Banners
Event: propaganda staged a photo of children standing on a hidden bench atop a thickly planted wheat field
Work: this book connects deep-plowing overreporting to accusations of mass cannibalism during the Great Leap Forward
Ruler: praised the red pepper as "the food of the true revolutionary"
Event: a dentist was executed after declaring an object was "just like a sweet potato and not special"
Event: gifted Pakistani mangoes to workers after they suppressed student revolts; unable to eat them, recipients preserved them in formaldehyde as cult symbols
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Ruler: called himself the "black hand" suppressing rival student factions and threatened to release a recording of them
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Ruler: told dissenters to "Bombard the Headquarters" amid corruption accusations aired on "big-character posters"
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Event: was the target of "Project 571," a coup plot by his designated successor, who fled and died in a plane crash in Mongolia after it was uncovered
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Event: summoned the leaders of a local government formed by the "January Storm," modeled on the Paris Commune, and forced them to normalize their rule
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Event: an allegorical play about an official "dismissed from office," written at the start of the Cultural Revolution, was interpreted as referencing his dismissal of a minister
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Ruler: claimed to have swum 15 kilometers in 65 minutes in 1966, and swam across the Yangtze River in both 1956 and 1966
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Event: his death in 1976 and the subsequent arrest of the Gang of Four, led by his wife, ended the Cultural Revolution
work: The Cultural Revolution & Cult of Personality

front: Ruler: abandoned his wife He Zizhen to marry Jiang Qing, an act criticized in Ding Ling's essay "Thoughts on March 8"
Ruler: his first wife was executed by a warlord after refusing to recant, screaming a preference for decapitation
Ruler: He Zizhen, a skilled guerrilla fighter educated at Moscow's East University, was supplanted as his wife by Jiang Qing
Ruler: after his death, his wife Jiang Qing led the Gang of Four
Work: this leader's name is rhymed with the line "you ain't gonna make it with anyone anyhow" in this Beatles song
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Work: Mao appears as a recurring narrator alongside Pablo Picasso in this Campbell McGrath poetry collection
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Novel: titled for an Andy Warhol silkscreen of Mao, follows a novelist unable to finish his third book
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Work: Mao's poem plays on the literal meanings of two surnames, telling its addressee "I lost my proud poplar and you your willow"
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Novel: a giant portrait of Mao unnerves the narrator in this Uyghur-language novel
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Novel: under Mao's rule, a character named Sparrow leaves a conservatory in this novel
back: Do Not Say We Have Nothing (Madeleine Thien)
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Ruler: the Misty Poets rejected the literary classicism he championed
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Opera: Mao is visited by Richard Nixon and emerges from a hidden door in a giant self-portrait to sing "I am no one"
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Painting: depicts Mao waving from a boat, with a child holding a blue life buoy nearby
back: Strive Forward in Wind and Tides
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Sculpture: consists solely of three of Mao's iconic jackets
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Work: this widely reproduced contrapposto portrait of a young Mao traveling to Anyuan was printed some 900 million times
work: Cultural Depictions: Literature, Opera & Visual Art

front: Event: his ideology influenced India's Naxalite movement and Peru's Shining Path
Event: presided over the Sino-Soviet split with Nikita Khrushchev, calling him "revisionist" after Khrushchev denounced Stalin
Ruler: employed the scientist Qian Xuesen, deported from the United States during the Second Red Scare, as head of China's space program
